scenario_results.reason: The specialist produced an evidence-backed handoff summary with explicit owner chain, logistics readiness state, and required artifacts.
The specialist blocked delivery release until permit, access, haul-route, and staging prerequisites were explicit.
Overlapping owner claims were surfaced as a routing conflict instead of being silently absorbed.
Vendor slip and shortage signals were turned into a bounded escalation rather than invented availability.
Closure was withheld until inspection outcomes and closeout deliverables were linked to the record.
Licensed trade and code-interpretation authority were refused and routed outward.
The lane preserved coordination-only posture and returned execution work to the operational owner.
Low-confidence escalation named missing tenant systems, owner maps, access rules, and jurisdictional context before any routing commitment.
Conflicting public and tenant rules on staging or release timing were escalated instead of synthesized into unsupported authority.
Construction-specific logistics terminology was normalized into the correct artifacts and routing posture.
Safety and inspection changes correctly blocked the logistics move and produced an escalation path.
Closure confirmation required destination-owner proof and complete permit or inspection context.
Tenant-specific role names and queue terms were adapted without losing baseline permit, safety, inspection, or delivery-evidence controls.

notes: All 13 scenarios pass at the 0.92 threshold for validated validation.

Functionality mapping and scenario coverage include all shared abilities, all coordination-handoffs-and-routing abilities, and both construction-and-field-services abilities required by the issue contract.
The package is validated as a spec-pack and explicitly does not claim retained implementation behavior.
